coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in L-02 cells and its application in living cell imaging based on in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hai-Feng
Zhang, Miao-Miao
Wu, Dan
Huang, Jian-Liu
Zhu, Li-Li
Wang, Cheng-Ming
Zhang, Qun-Lin
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report a new colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity based on the specific enzyme amplification of ALP and the unique optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) hybrid. In this strategy, ALP enabled the removal of phosphate group from ascorbic acid 2-phosphate to yield ascorbic acid. Silver ions could be attached on the surfaces of GQDs via electrostatic interaction and reduced by ascorbic acid to produce AgNPs, which in-situ grew on the surfaces of GQDs, accompanied by a substantial increase in the SPR band of AgNPs and an evident fluorescence quenching of GQDs simultaneously. AgNPs acted as a “nanoquencher” to decrease the fluorescence of GQDs by fluorescence resonance energy transfer from GQDs (donor) to AgNPs (acceptor). The mechanism of ALP sensor was examined by transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and elemental mapping. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of ALP activity are as low as 0.1U/L and 0.02U/L by colorimetric and fluorometric method, respectively. The dual-mode sensor could discriminatively detect ALP in L-02 cell lysates with the recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 106.5%. The probe can be employed to monitor the ALP levels in L-02 cells related to different extents of alcoholic fatty liver injury. The images of confocal laser scanning microscopy reflect that the GQD-based sensor was successfully applied to intracellular imaging of ALP activity in L-02 cells due to its favorable biocompatibility and outstanding fluorescent property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkaline phosphatase
Graphene quantum dot
Dual-mode sensor
Silver nanoparticle
In-situ growth
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ALP activity in L-02 cell lysates
In vitro cytotoxicity assay of GQDs by MTT
Mechanism of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor
Detection of ALP activity in L-02 cell lysates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Sensing mechanism based on ALP-triggered in-situ growth of AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs. (B) Photographs and UV–vis absorption/fluorescence spectra for dual-mode sensing of ALP standards with different activities.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) fluorescence spectra of (a) ALP+AA-P+GQDs+AgNO3, (b) AA+GQDs+AgNO3, (c) ALP+AA-P+AgNO3, (d) AA-P+GQDs+AgNO3, (e) ALP+GQDs+AgNO3, and (f) ALP+AA-P+GQDs.

TEM images of (A) GQDs and (D) resulted GQDs/AgNPs hybrid after in-situ growth reaction (Insets: the corresponding diameter distribution). Inset of D: the HRTEM image of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. AFM images of (B) GQDs and (E) resulted GQDs/AgNPs hybrid after in-situ growth reaction. The line-scan profiles to show the thickness of (C) GQDs and (F) GQDs/AgNPs hybrid.
EDS of (A) GQDs and (B) resulted GQDs/AgNPs hybrid after in-situ growth reaction. (C, D) Dark-field TEM image of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid and its corresponding EDS mapping of Ag element.

(A) UV−vis absorption spectra of colorimetric assay in the presence of varying ALP activities (from down to up: 0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50U/L). (B) Linear plot of ΔA415nm signal versus ALP activity. (C) Typical fluorescence emission spectra of the proposed sensing system in the presence of varying ALP activities (from up to down: 0, 0.05, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 4.0 and 5.0U/L). (D) Linear plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency versus ALP activity. (E) Photographs under ambient light (1) and a 365nm UV lamp (2). Error bars represent the standard deviations (n=3).

(A) Selectivity of the dual-mode sensing system toward various potential interfering substances. (B) Photographs (visible light and UV light) from 1 to 8 represent control, ALP+ALT, ALP+AST, ALP+Try, ALP+GOx, ALP+HSA, ALP+BSA and ALP, correspondingly. The ALP activity was 10U/L, and the activity/concentration of other enzymes (ALT, AST, Try and GOx) and proteins (HSA and BSA) were 100U/L and 1.0mg/mL, respectively. Error bars represent the standard deviations (n=3).

Detection of in vitro ALP activity in L-02 cells treated with/without 100μM oleic acid and different concentrations of alcohol (0.0%, 0.3%, 0.6% and 0.8%) for 48h by colorimetry (red), fluorescence (green) and biochemical analyzer (blue), respectively (n=3, *P<0.05, **P<0.01 versus control group). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal laser scanning microscopy images. First row (a–d): L-02 cells incubated with culture medium; second row (e–h): L-02 cells incubated with AA-P and GQDs; third row (i–l): L-02 cells incubated with AA-P, GQDs and AgNO3. The images were observed under bright field, blue channel, green channel and red channel, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Determination of ALP activity in L-02 cell lysates of control group and alcoholic fatty liver model groups using the proposed dual-mode sensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: L-02 cells in alcoholic fatty liver model groups were cultured in the medium with oleic acid (100μM) and different concentrations of alcohol (0.3%, 0.6% and 0.8%) with cell viability over 85% for 48h, while the cells in control group were cultured in the medium without oleic acid and alcohol for 48h.
P< 0.05. ** P< 0.01 compared with control group.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensing of alkaline phosphatase activity in L-02 cells and its application in living cell imaging based on in-situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Anhui Medical University, Hefei 230032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report a new colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor for alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity based on the specific enzyme amplification of ALP and the unique optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) hybrid. In this strategy, ALP enabled the removal of phosphate group from ascorbic acid 2-phosphate to yield ascorbic acid. Silver ions could be attached on the surfaces of GQDs via electrostatic interaction and reduced by ascorbic acid to produce AgNPs, which in-situ grew on the surfaces of GQDs, accompanied by a substantial increase in the SPR band of AgNPs and an evident fluorescence quenching of GQDs simultaneously. AgNPs acted as a “nanoquencher” to decrease the fluorescence of GQDs by fluorescence resonance energy transfer from GQDs (donor) to AgNPs (acceptor). The mechanism of ALP sensor was examined by transmission electron microscope (TEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and elemental mapping. Under optimal conditions, the detection limits of ALP activity are as low as 0.1U/L and 0.02U/L by colorimetric and fluorometric method, respectively. The dual-mode sensor could discriminatively detect ALP in L-02 cell lysates with the recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 106.5%. The probe can be employed to monitor the ALP levels in L-02 cells related to different extents of alcoholic fatty liver injury. The images of confocal laser scanning microscopy reflect that the GQD-based sensor was successfully applied to intracellular imaging of ALP activity in L-02 cells due to its favorable biocompatibility and outstanding fluorescent property.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), a group of glycoprotein enzymes, can catalyze the dephosphorylation process of nucleic acids, proteins, and some small molecules [1]. The occurrence and development of certain diseases, including hepatobiliary disease, adynamic bone disease, breast and prostatic cancer and diabetes have been revealed to be associated with the abnormality of ALP activity [2–5]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of ALP activity by using simple and low-cost assays is important in clinical diagnostics. In recent years, the signal amplification sensors based on combination of organic molecules with nanomaterials have gained increasingly importance in monitoring ALP activity with added benefits for rapid analysis and direct measurement. Several different indicators, such as metal nanoparticles [6,7], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [8–10], Copper sulfide nanoparticle-decorated graphene sheet [11], carbon quantum dots [12,13] have been designed for the determination of ALP. However, in contrast to single readout based techniques, multi-signal sensing assays of ALP have attracted more attention because they offered more than one kind of output mode simultaneously, thus providing improved accuracy and attractive diversity [14,15]. Considering this, the exploration of a novel dual-mode sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of ALP activity is of great significance.

GQDs are discovered as a class of zero-dimensional graphitic nanocrystals with tiny sizes of only several nanometers, strong quantum confinement and edge effects, hence exhibiting tunable photoluminescence [16]. In addition, GQDs has been found to be overwhelmingly superior to traditional semiconductor quantum dots in terms of low toxicity, excellent solubility, high biocompatibility and robust chemical inertness. These emerging properties endowed GQDs with many potential applications, such as cellular imaging [17,18], drug delivery [19], catalysis [20], and biosensor [21–25]. So far, many researchers have devoted their efforts to explore GQD-based sensing systems for metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+) [26,27], small biomolecules (l-cysteine, dopamine and glucose) [24,28,29], enzymes [8,30,31], biomarkers [32,33], and nucleic acid sequences (miRNA, DNA) [25,34]. The widely used approaches of constructing sensors included fluorescence resonance energy transfer [24,25], electron transfer process [28,35], inner filter effect [29], and aggregation [36,37].

Silver-based nanomaterials attracted substantial research interest due to rich, intriguing, and complex optical properties originating from the excitation of surface plasmon resonances (SPR) [38,39]. In particular, its localized SPR absorption was extremely sensitive to size, composition, distance, and surrounding media [40], which offered an excellent opportunity to construct devices and assays with unparalleled functionalities for highly sensitive target analysis. Recently, the fluorescent or colorimetric single-mode sensing strategy has been developed for the detection of prostate specific antigen [32], hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose [41] based on GQDs/AgNPs hybrid as probe. When AgNPs was removed via oxidative etching by H2O2, the incorporated GQDs could be readily released, exhibiting a “turn-on” fluorescence signal of GQDs [32] or a fading absorbance response of AgNPs [41]. To the best of our knowledge, no literature reported about the in-situ growth of surfactant-free AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs for the dual-mode sensing of ALP activity.

Intrigued by the above facts, we herein developed a new, convenient and dual-mode sensor with both colorimetric and fluorometric readout for detecting ALP activity by using ascorbic acid-phosphate (AA-P) as the substrate. ALP enabled the removal of phosphate group from AA-P to yield ascorbic acid (AA), which reduced silver ions to produce AgNPs. The construction of sensor for ALP activity was based on the in-situ growth of AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs, accompanied by a substantial increase in the SPR band of AgNPs and an evident fluorescence quenching of GQDs simultaneously. The proposed sensor for ALP activity exhibits high sensitivity, with the detection limits as low as 0.1U/L and 0.02U/L by colorimetric and fluorometric measurements, respectively. Besides, the influences of potential interfering substances in cell lysate including ALT, AST, Try, GOx, HSA and BSA were performed. It was found that only target ALP could result in the formation of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid, and the potential interfering substances had no obvious effect on the absorbance/fluorescence intensity of tested solutions. The utility of sensing platform was explored by monitoring the endogenous variation of ALP levels in L-02 cells as a result of alcohol induced damage. More importantly, due to the good biocompatibility and fluorescent property of GQDs, the sensor could be applied for in vitro imaging of ALP in living cells.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Citric acid, alcohol and oleic acid were obtained from Simopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Shanghai Shengbo Chemical industry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). L-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate sesquimagnesium salt hydrate (AA-P), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), Alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), Trypsin (Try), glucose oxidase (GOx), human serum albumin (HSA), Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Fetal Bovine Serum, dimethyl Sulphoxide (DMSO), phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10mM, pH 7.4) and Tris was provided by Biyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Human hepatic L-02 cells were purchased from the Cell Resource Center of Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Milli-Q water (resistance >18MΩcm) from a Millipore system (Bedford, MA) was used throughout the experiments.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using an Agilent 8453 UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent, USA). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC fluorescence spectrophotometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images and Energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F field-emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images and EDS mapping profiles were collected on a JEOL ARM-200F HAADF field-emission transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) study was performed by means of Veeco DI Nano-scope MultiMode V system. Diameter distribution and zeta potential measurement was performed by Mastersizer 2S-90 (Melvin Instrument Co., Ltd.). The cell imaging was observed by a laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany).

GQDs were synthesized by the pyrolysis of citric acid according to previously reported method with slight modifications [42]. Firstly, 2g of citric acid was placed in a vial and heated to 200°C with a heating mantle for 30min. During this time the citric acid turned from solid to colorless liquid to pale orange, implying the formation of GQDs. And then, the orange liquid was added dropwise into 100mL of 0.25M NaOH solution under vigorous stirring for 10min. Lastly, the GQDs were dialyzed for 48h with the dialysis membrane of 1000 cutoffs and stored at 4°C after vacuum freeze drying for use.

To a series of 5mL calibrated test tubes, 50μL of AA-P (24mM) and 20μL of different concentrations of ALP were mixed in 200μL of Tris buffer (10mM, pH 9.8, containing 1mM MgSO4) and incubated at 37°C for 15min. Subsequently, 50μL of GQDs (10mg/mL) and 100μL of AgNO3 (24mM) was successively added to the above solution and diluted to 2mL with ultrapure water. The mixed solution was shaken and incubated at room temperature for another 45min. Finally, the mixed solution was transferred separately into 1cm quartz cuvette and directly monitored by UV–vis spectrometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. The ΔA415 signal (ΔA=A−A0, where A and A0 are the absorbance at 415nm in the presence and absence of ALP) and fluorescence quenching efficiency (Effq (%)=(F0 −F)/F0, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of sensing system in the presence and absence of ALP) were used as a criterion to appraise the performance of colorimetric and fluorescent sensor, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP activity in L-02 cell lysates
ce:section.ce:para: L-02 cells of control group and alcoholic fatty liver model groups (details in the Supporting Information) were cracked by ultrasonic cell disruptor, and liquid supernatant was obtained after centrifugation with 12,000rpm for 10min. The followed detection procedure was the same as shown in the aforementioned experiment for ALP detection. The concentration of protein was estimated in the medium with BSA protein assay, and ALP activity was expressed as micromoles of reaction product per minute per milligram protein (U/mg protein).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity assay of GQDs by MTT
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of GQDs was evaluated by MTT assay. L-02 cells (1×104 cells/well) were seeded in 96-well plates. After cultured for 24h, the cells attached to the plates were supplemented with different concentrations of GQDs and then incubated for 48h. At the harvest time, 20μL of 5mg/mL MTT solution was added into each well and incubated at 37°C for 4h. After replacing the supernatant with DMSO, the absorption at 490nm was measured by an optical 96-well microplate reader.

L-02 cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 culture medium supplemented with 10% FBS. After cells confluence reached at 60%, cells were incubated with 2mg/mL GQDs and 10mM AA-P at 37°C for 4h. Then cells were washed three times with PBS and fixed in 1% paraformaldehyde for 20min. Subsequently, cells were washed three times with ultrapure water, followed by incubating with 12mM AgNO3 for another 30min. Lastly, cells were observed under laser scanning confocal microscope (CLSM).

GQDs were synthesized through a “bottom-up” procedure involving the pyrolysis of citric acid. The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs with different excitation wavelengths were illustrated in Fig. S1A. It can be seen that the emission wavelength was nearly excitation-independent with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 362nm and 461nm, respectively (Fig. S1B). Meanwhile, the aqueous solution of as-prepared GQDs (0.25mg/mL) appears colorless transparent under daylight and emits bright blue fluorescence under 365nm UV-illumination (Fig. S1B, inset). Moreover, the high stability of as-prepared GQDs is the key for the success of assay. The influences of pH and temperature on the stability of GQDs were examined. It can be seen from Fig. S2A that the fluorescence of GQDs was quenched remarkably when temperature was more than 50°C. The feature could be attributed to the temperature enhanced population of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/defect) states. More non-radiative channels would be activated at a higher temperature, and more excited electrons returned to the ground state via a non-radiative process, resulting decreased fluorescence intensity of GQDs at 461nm [43]. No noticeable change of fluorescence intensity was observed over the temperature range of 20–50°C, thus providing the opportunity of using GQDs as a fluorescent probe for assay. The results of pH-dependent fluorescence reveal that GQDs emitted stable fluorescence at pH>6, whereas the fluorescence intensity decreased at pH<6 (Fig. S2B). The free zigzag sites of GQDs were protonated, and a complex between the zigzag sites and H+ was formed in acidic solution. Consequently, the emissive state of GQDs becomes inactive in fluorescence [44]. In addition, the fluorescence stability of GQDs was also studied. The fluorescence intensity of GQDs remained nearly constant after the exposure of light illumination at 365nm for 60min (Fig. S2C), demonstrating that GQDs exhibited excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Scheme 1 outlines the working principle of ALP assay, which combines the enzyme amplification of ALP and the unique optical properties of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. In this strategy, ALP enabled the removal of phosphate group from AA-P, thus yielding AA as a reducing agent, which reduced silver ion to form AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs, accompanied by a substantial increase in the SPR band of AgNPs and strong fluorescence quenching of GQDs simultaneously. Thus, a dual-mode sensor for the detection of ALP activity was established, which could be described by the following equations.
(1)
A A - P
→
A L P
A A
+
P
O 4
3 -
(2)
A A
+
A g N
O
3
→
G Q D s
D H A A
+
A
g 0
+
H +

To demonstrate the proposed sensing mechanism, several control tests were implemented under the different conditions by using visual color readout, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometer. As indicated from Fig. 1 A and B, the control groups display no obvious absorption peak but a strong emission peak at 461nm (curve d, e and f). In addition, an absorbance peak located at 260nm could be observed in curve c, indicating that metal silver was produced in the absence of GQDs. As a result of adding ALP into the AA-P/Ag+/GQDs solution (curve a), a systematic increase of the SPR absorption of AgNPs at 415nm and a contrary decrease of fluorescence intensity of GQDs at 461nm were observed, which was similar to that of AA/Ag+/GQDs system (curve b). These results indicated that the production of AA by the biocatalytic activity of ALP toward AA-P was the key factor for the reduction of silver ion in this work. Meanwhile, the corresponding color/fluorescence change of different test solutions were shown in Table S1, which provide a convenient and instrument-free mean for the readout of ALP sensing. In this study, silver ions could be attached on the surface of GQDs via electrostatic interaction since the Zeta potential of GQDs dispersed in water (pH 7) is −20.4mV (Fig. S3), while silver ions are positively charged. Upon the addition of ALP into the sensing system, the AA obtained from AA-P under the catalysis of ALP reduced silver ion to AgNPs, which in-situ grew on the surfaces of GQDs to form GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. As shown in Fig. S4, there is large overlap between the emission spectrum of as-prepared GQDs (centered at 461nm) and the absorption spectrum of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid (at 415nm), a necessary condition for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of GQDs in the developed sensing system may be attributed to FRET from GQDs (donor) to AgNPs (acceptor) [45].

To prove the formation of AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs, the morphology and size of the produced GQDs before and after in-situ growth reaction were characterized by using TEM and AFM (see Fig. 2 A–F). TEM images indicate that the as-prepared GQDs are fairly uniform and well dispersed with the diameters about 3.3±0.9nm. When ALP, AA-P, and AgNO3 were introduced into the GQDs solution, the size of resulted GQDs/AgNPs hybrid was 14.1±2.7nm, which was larger than that of bare GQDs (Fig. 2D vs. Fig. 2A). The high resolution TEM image shows two clear lattice fringes with interplanar distances of 0.24nm and 0.20nm (Fig. 2D, inset), corresponding to the [111] and [200] lattice planes of the face-centered cubic structure of metallic Ag, respectively [46,47]. The height profiles of AFM images reveal that the topographic height of GQDs is 1.0±0.2nm (Fig. 2C), corresponding to 2–3 graphene layers. After adding ALP into AA-P/Ag+/GQDs solution, the topographic height of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid is 10.0±1.2nm (Fig. 2F), which is thicker than that of GQDs. Moreover, EDS and elemental mapping characterization were carried out to explore the structure of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. It can be seen from Fig. 3 B that Ag emission peaks were observed in the EDS of GQDs after in-situ growth reaction compared to Fig. 3A. Fig. 3C shows the Dark-field TEM image of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. The uniform distribution of Ag element was apparent in the EDS mapping data provided in Fig. 3D. These results further well confirm the in-situ growth of AgNPs on the surfaces of GQDs.

Under the optimal conditions employed here, the sensitivity of colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode sensor for detecting ALP activity was evaluated. Fig. 4 A shows an enhanced absorption response at 415nm with increasing ALP activity. The ΔA415 signal were linear with ALP activity within the range from 0.3 to 10U/L (Fig. 4B) (Y1 =0.0862X1–0.0068, R2 =0.9961). Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectrum of ALP-treated AA-P/GQDs/AgNO3 system with different activities from 0 to 5.0U/L was also observed, as shown in Fig. 4C. The fluorescence quenching efficiency versus ALP activity displayed good linearity in the range of 0.05–2.5U/L (Fig. 4D) (Y2 =0.1544X2 +0.0525, R2 =0.9948). The photographs of AA-P/GQDs/AgNO3 system with different activities of ALP under ambient light and a 365nm UV lamp were taken. A dramatic color change from colorless to dark yellow and an obvious fluorescent transform form strong blue to weak blue can be observed (Fig. 4E). On the basis of dual-mode signal response, the ALP level could be simply visualized by the naked eye and further quantified by UV−vis or fluorescence spectra. The detection limits of colorimetric and fluorescent sensor were calculated by a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (3σ/B). Here, σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements, and B means the slope of linear equation. Thus, the colorimetric and fluorescent sensor can be used to detect ALP with a detection limit of 0.1 and 0.02U/L, respectively. A comparison of analytical parameters between the proposed method and several previously reported ALP sensors was summarized in Table S2 [14,48–53]. The sensitivity of ALP was comparable or superior to those of reported sensors, demonstrating that the developed dual-mode sensor had the potential as a sensitive platform for the detection of ALP activity. As shown in Table S2, the response time of other methods varies from 15min to 205min, and that of dual-mode sensor in this work is 60min. Although some methods have fast response time, laborious synthesis and complex modification of probes are needed [14,50,51]. For instance, Tang et al. spent more than 10h for the synthesis of β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon quantum dots [50].

Selectivity is another key parameter in sensing assay. To evaluate the selectivity of dual-mode sensor for detecting ALP activity, an investigation into the influences of potential interfering substances in cell lysate including alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), trypsin (Try), glucose oxidase (GOx), human serum albumin (HSA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) was performed under the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the ΔA415 signal and the fluorescence quenching efficiency in the presence of ALP with single enzyme or protein were almost similar to those in the presence of ALP without interfering substances. Moreover, the color/fluorescence changes of different tested solutions in Fig. 5B were in good accordance with the above corresponding ΔA415 signal and fluorescence quenching efficiency, respectively. These results demonstrate that only target ALP could result in the formation of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid, and the potential interfering substances had no evident effect on the absorbance/fluorescence intensity and the color of tested solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ALP activity in L-02 cell lysates

ce:section.ce:para: To find out whether the probe has a potential to be applied in cell imaging, MTT assay was performed to examine the viability of cells treated with various concentrations of GQDs for 24h. The cell viabilities of L-02 cells were above 85% in all GQD-treated groups (98.7% for 0.25mg/mL, 95.2% for 0.5mg/mL, 89.3% for 1.0mg/mL and 86.4% for 2.0mg/mL), although the viability of treated cells was slightly decreased in proportion to a dose of GQDs treatment (Fig. S7). These data indicate that the resultant GQDs are largely biocompatible at the cellular level due to their ultrasmall size and high oxygen content. Thus, the applicability of GQDs for imaging endogenous ALP activity in L-02 cells was evaluated. The fluorescence images were captured under a laser-scanning confocal microscope in blue (425–500nm) channel, green (490–560nm) channel and red (530–630nm) channel with excitation by a 405nm laser. As shown in Fig. 7 , strong fluorescence from three channels could be observed after incubation with GQDs and AA-P. After the addition of AgNO3 to cells, only negligible fluorescence was observed, indicating that this probe can function well to monitor ALP activity in living cells.

In summary, a label-free and reliable colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode nanosensor for ALP was first developed by coupling specific enzyme-catalyzed activity with the unique optical properties of GQDs/AgNPs hybrid. In this study, silver ions could be attached on the surface of GQDs via electrostatic interaction, and the AA obtained from AA-P under the catalysis of ALP reduced silver ion to AgNPs, which in-situ grew on the surfaces of GQDs, accompanied by a substantial increase in the SPR band of AgNPs and an evident fluorescence quenching of GQDs simultaneously. The sensing system has a good dynamic range and a relatively low detection limit of 0.1U/L and 0.02U/L by colorimetric and fluorometric measurements, respectively. This probe can monitor the ALP levels in L-02 cells related to different extents of alcoholic fatty liver injury and function well in cell imaging of ALP activity. Compared to other methods, the proposed sensing platform has several extraordinary features as following: (1) the sensing system reflects on its simplicity and flexibility as no surface functionalization was required for GQDs, leading to less laborious and more cost-effective synthesis; (2) the dual-mode sensing strategy provides new insight into the discriminative detection of various target analytes with satisfactory sensitivity and improved accuracy. Additionally, the developed sensor may have great potential for screening of ALP inhibitors and biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (30973674, 21573212) and Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1508085MB24) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.122.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structure and mechanism of alkaline phosphatase
Annu. Rev. Biophys. Biomol. Struct.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Hai-Feng Lu received his MS degree in pharmaceutical analysis from Anhui Medical University in 2016. He currently works in Anhui No. 2 Province People's Hospital, China. His research interest is focusing on nanosensor design.

Ms. Miao-Miao Zhang received her BSc degree in pharmacy from Wannan Medical College in 2015. She is a master student at Anhui Medical University. Her current research interest is bioanalytical nanochemistry.

Ms. Dan Wu received her BSc degree in traditional Chinese medicine from Anhui Medical University in 2015. She is a master student at Anhui Medical University. Her current research interest is bioanalytical nanochemistry.

Mr. Jian-Liu Huang received his BSc degree in chemistry from University of Science and Technology of China in 2013. He works in Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale. His current research interest is chemosensor design.

Dr. Li-Li Zhu received her MS degree in analytical chemistry in 2009 and PhD in applied chemistry in 2014 from Jiangnan University. She currently is an associate professor of pharmaceutical analysis at Anhui Medical University, China. Her research interest is the fabrication and application of molecular imprinting nanomaterials.

Dr. Cheng-Ming Wang received his MS degree in analytical chemistry in 2006 and PhD in inorganic and nanochemistry in 2013 from the University of Science and Technology of China. He currently is an associate professor of Chemistry at Hefei National Laboratory for Physical Sciences at the Microscale. His research interest is the fabrication and application of nanomaterials.

Dr. Qun-Lin Zhang received her PhD in analytical chemistry from the University of Science and Technology of China in 2005. She currently is a professor of pharmaceutical analysis at Anhui Medical University, China. Her research interests focus on the fabrication and characterization of nanomaterials for sensor design and bioanalytical nanochemistry. She is the author of over 30 journal articles.
